items.q: How does the sign of cross elasticity of demand (XED) reveal product relationships?
items.correct: Positive XED = substitutes (price of one rises, demand for the other rises); negative XED = complements (price of one rises, demand for the other falls); zero XED = unrelated goods.
items.misconception: The sign of XED doesn't matter — only the size does.
items.random_wrong: XED measures how often consumers cross-shop between stores.
items.why: The sign carries the qualitative information about the relationship — substitute vs complement. Size tells you the strength of the relationship. Students focus on magnitude and miss the categorical meaning of the sign.

items.q: What is the difference between CPI and RPI as inflation measures?
items.correct: Both measure price changes via a basket of goods, but RPI includes housing costs (mortgage interest) and uses an arithmetic mean; CPI excludes housing costs and uses a geometric mean — CPI is the official UK target measure.
items.misconception: CPI and RPI always give the same inflation figure.
items.random_wrong: CPI measures consumer prices and RPI measures retail prices — that's the only difference.
items.why: RPI usually runs higher than CPI due to formula and coverage differences — this matters hugely for pensions, rail fares and benefits. Students treat the two as identical and miss the policy implications of which is used.

items.correct: A cost imposed on a third party not involved in the transaction — e.g. pollution from a factory affects neighbours who didn't choose to bear it.
items.misconception: A negative externality is something bad that happens to a business.
items.random_wrong: A negative externality is a tax on a business.
items.why: The 'externality' is external to the market participants — borne by THIRD parties. Students miss the structure: producer, consumer, then someone else who didn't agree to be affected.
Correct answer: Money the bank pays you. “A type of tax” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.correct: A long loan to buy a house
items.why: It's A long loan to buy a house. “A current account” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Save £200 at 10% for one year. Total, in £?
items.why: The real figure is 220. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
